skill.name: SKILL: structure_judgment
skill.description: Front-end structural routing for mixed, ambiguous, high-noise inputs. This skill analyzes user inputs to determine what kind of structural problem they present and routes them to the appropriate handling layer.
skill.summary: Route complex AI inputs to optimal response layers by identifying structural problems before answering.
skill.author: SyntagmaNull

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill containing no executable code. The static analysis scanned 0 files (0 lines) and detected 0 potential security issues. The skill provides routing instructions for AI models to handle complex inputs more effectively. No dangerous patterns, no network access, no filesystem operations, and no external command execution are present in this prompt-based skill.

content.user_title: Route Complex Inputs to Optimal Response Layers
content.value_statement: AI models often answer the wrong layer of complex user inputs, leading to drift, overreach, or fake certainty. Structure judgment analyzes inputs to identify the primary structural problem and routes to the correct handling layer before responding.
actual_capabilities: Identifies structural layers in user inputs: FACT, INTERPRETATION, EVALUATION, ACTION, STATE, EVIDENCE_CONFLICT, VERIFICATION_NEED
Determines the primary layer that must be stabilized first to prevent bad answers
Detects structural hazards including premise-smuggling, layer-collapse, escalation drift, and validation capture
Routes inputs to appropriate downstream skills like judgment_hygiene or verification_hygiene
Handles safety triage for inputs containing self-harm signals or crisis language
Manages multimodal inputs by reading text and non-text channels independently
limitations: Does not provide final answers; only routes inputs to the correct layer
Does not substitute for factual verification; triggers verification when needed
Requires integration with downstream skills like judgment_hygiene for full effectiveness
Does not guarantee correct final judgment or factual truth on its own

use_cases.title: Handling emotionally charged escalation requests
use_cases.description: When a user asks 'should I confront my boss now?' with loaded premises embedded, route to stabilize interpretation before action.
use_cases.target_user: AI assistants responding to workplace conflict questions

use_cases.title: Multimodal input analysis
use_cases.description: When user text describes an image differently than what the image shows, identify the evidence conflict before answering.

use_cases.title: Self-harm signal triage
use_cases.description: When input contains crisis language, perform safety triage to determine whether immediate response is needed versus bounded acknowledgment.
use_cases.target_user: AI assistants handling sensitive mental health topics

prompt_templates.title: Basic Input Classification
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this input and identify: 1) What is the primary structural layer (FACT, INTERPRETATION, EVALUATION, ACTION, STATE, EVIDENCE_CONFLICT, or VERIFICATION_NEED)? 2) What is the secondary layer? 3) What is the main structural hazard? Input: {user_input}
prompt_templates.scenario: When you receive a complex or ambiguous user message and need to determine how to structure your response

prompt_templates.title: Escalation Request Routing
prompt_templates.prompt: The user is asking for advice about a consequential action. Before answering: 1) Identify any embedded premises being smuggled as facts. 2) Determine if FACT or INTERPRETATION needs stabilization first. 3) Name the main hazard (escalation drift, validation capture, etc.). Input: {escalation_query}
prompt_templates.scenario: When users ask 'should I do X?' with emotionally loaded framing

prompt_templates.title: Multimodal Conflict Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: For this multimodal input: 1) Read the non-text channel independently first. 2) Identify what the text claims versus what the image/audio shows. 3) Determine if EVIDENCE_CONFLICT is the primary layer. Input: {multimodal_input}
prompt_templates.scenario: When processing image-text or audio-text pairs where channels may conflict

prompt_templates.title: Safety Signal Triage
prompt_templates.prompt: A safety signal has been detected. Perform triage: 1) Check specificity - is this vague distress or concrete act reference? 2) Check immediacy - is danger framed as now/tonight/immediate? 3) Check method linkage - is a method/tool mentioned? 4) Determine routing: Case A (immediate risk), Case B (high-distress nonspecific), or Case C (low-specificity/ambiguous).
prompt_templates.scenario: When input contains potential self-harm signals, suicide references, or crisis language

output_examples.input: "My boss is obviously building a case to fire me. Should I CC his boss now?"
output_examples.output: Primary layer: INTERPRETATION | Secondary layer: ACTION | Main hazard: premise-smuggling + escalation drift | Route: Stabilize interpretation ('building a case') as candidate interpretation, not fact, before addressing escalation action.

output_examples.input: Image shows mild finding; user text says "this proves I'm dying."
output_examples.output: Primary layer: EVIDENCE_CONFLICT | Secondary layer: STATE | Main hazard: text-anchoring bias | Route: Parse image independently first. Treat user text as candidate interpretation, not anchor reality. Contain the self-totalizing conclusion.

output_examples.input: "Is this medicine still approved for children in France?"
output_examples.output: Primary layer: VERIFICATION_NEED | Secondary layer: FACT | Main hazard: verification bypass | Route: Trigger verification_hygiene before judgment. Answer depends on current external regulatory facts.

best_practices: Identify the primary layer that must be stabilized first - do not assume a fixed global priority order for all inputs
Read multimodal inputs (text + image/audio) independently before combining - never let text pre-interpret non-text channels
Treat embedded conclusions in user wording as candidate INTERPRETATION or STATE, not as FACT - avoid inheriting premise-smuggled language as proven fact
anti_patterns: Answering ACTION requests before stabilizing FACT or INTERPRETATION layers - this causes escalation drift
Treating the user's emotionally loaded wording as already-proven fact rather than candidate interpretation
Using meta-language like 'there are multiple layers here' without actually changing the answer order or routing

faq.question: What is structure judgment?
faq.answer: Structure judgment is a routing skill that analyzes user inputs to identify what kind of structural problem they present. It determines which layer (FACT, INTERPRETATION, ACTION, etc.) must be handled first to prevent bad answers.

faq.question: Do I need other skills with structure_judgment?
faq.answer: Yes. Structure judgment is a routing layer that should be paired with judgment_hygiene for output structure discipline and verification_hygiene when external evidence is needed.

faq.question: Does this skill see the final answer?
faq.answer: No. Structure judgment is a front-end routing skill that runs before judgment_hygiene and before search/verification workflows. It decides what kind of problem the input is, not the final answer.

faq.question: What are the main structural hazards?
faq.answer: The key hazards include: premise-smuggling (conclusion hidden as premise), layer-collapse (layers blended together), escalation drift (action answered before facts), verification bypass (answering from plausibility instead of checking), and text-anchoring bias (letting text control image interpretation).

faq.question: How does safety triage work?
faq.answer: When inputs contain self-harm signals or crisis language, the skill performs triage to determine: Case A (immediate risk - safety becomes primary), Case B (high-distress but nonspecific - preserve safety flag), or Case C (low-specificity/ambiguous - minimal acknowledgment but continue routing).

faq.question: What are the primary routing layers?
faq.answer: The seven layers are: FACT (claims about what happened), INTERPRETATION (claims about meaning or motive), EVALUATION (good/bad judgments), ACTION (requests or plans), STATE (speaker's internal condition), EVIDENCE_CONFLICT (channel/source mismatches), and VERIFICATION_NEED (external facts required).
